ICML 2026 — #4594
Fig. R1 Table R1 Fig. R2 Fig. R3 Table R2 Fig. R4 Fig. R5 Fig. R6 Fig. R7 Table R3 Table R4 Refs

Rebuttal for Submission #4594

Additional figures and tables referenced in the author response

This page accompanies the rebuttal and will be updated during the discussion period.

Figure R1 — Window size is determined by architectural constraints

Figure R1. The window size must balance two constraints: it should be large enough to capture the longest event, yet small enough for the model to reliably predict all events within it. F1 Event (IoU ≥ 0.5) is plotted as a function of window duration W, averaged across 5 cross-validation folds and 18 subjects. Two experimental settings are compared: (i) scaling the number of queries (Nq) proportionally with W, and (ii) fixing Nq = 150 while varying W.

Table R1 — Window configuration to enable sufficient resolution

DatasetW (s)Events / WinEvent Dur. (s)Tokens / Event
Broderick875.30.083.4
SparrKULee819.50.3013.1
Pinet819.30.1113.3
BI20133260.11.004.3
BI2014a3245.81.005.6
BI2014b89.91.0025.9
BNCI201481.34.00197
BNCI2015323.45.0074.4
TUAR1611.09.0423.2
TUSZ2560.2567.71024
Table R1. Tokens/Event must be greater than 3 to enable sufficient resolution for the model to predict all events. The Perceiver module resamples every window into 256 latent tokens regardless of duration. Tokens/Event is computed as 256 / n̄events, where n̄events is the mean number of events per window.

Figure R2 — F1event sensitivity to IoU threshold

F1event evolves smoothly across IoU thresholds. Curves are colored by task category (blue: Motor Imagery, green: Speech, purple: Typing, orange: Artifacts, red: P300, teal: Seizure). Shaded bands denote ±1 SEM across subjects.

Figure R3 — Decoupled metric for localization and temporal precision

DANCE is better on both metrics when the arrow points upper left. Joint view of detection quality (F1detect, y-axis) and temporal precision (center prediction error in seconds, x-axis, log scale) for DANCE (colored circles) versus the best non-ablation baseline (gray diamonds). Note Δctr is dependent on F1detect as it is only computed on matched events.

Table R2 — Decoupled detection and localization metrics

DANCEBest Baseline
DatasetBest BaselineF1detF1evtΔctr (s)F1detF1evtΔctr (s)
BNCI2014CBraMod0.9090.3980.0780.6270.2120.277
BNCI2015CBraMod0.8430.5730.1700.2890.1970.540
SparrKULeeCBraMod0.4110.4110.0740.1940.1940.806
BroderickCBraMod0.4460.4460.0140.1450.1450.260
PinetU-Net0.3540.3540.0210.0210.0210.038
TUARCBraMod0.1880.1450.2610.1720.1220.377
BI2013CBraMod0.6390.5000.1280.0160.0150.439
BI2014aCBraMod0.7040.5300.1250.0520.0480.376
BI2014bCBraMod0.5290.3920.1210.1040.0970.347
TUSZCBraMod0.3530.2104.5220.0210.0123.878
Table R2. DANCE outperforms the best baselines on both F1 metrics across all 10 datasets, while simultaneously achieving 2–18× lower center prediction errors on 9 out of 10 tasks. The table shows decoupled detection and localization metrics for DANCE and the best non-ablation baseline per dataset (IoU ≥ 0.5). F1detect measures class-agnostic event detection (“was an event found?”). F1event additionally requires correct classification. Δcenter is the mean absolute temporal error between predicted and ground-truth event centers, in seconds, computed over true positives.

Figure R4 — Onset-informed models fail to jointly detect and classify

Figure R4. DANCE outperforms EEGSym and ATCNet on BNCI2014 dataset. We evaluate two variants of EEGSym: classic, which produces a single class prediction per window and relies on timeline reconstruction to assign per-timestep labels, and dense, a minimal adaptation that outputs per-timestep predictions. We also added ATCNet, another strong model in the BCI literature. (A) F1 Sample (macro-averaged multiclass F1 over timesteps): DANCE achieves 50.8%, compared to 13.8%, 18.0%, and 15.2% for EEGSym classic, ATCNet, and EEGSym dense respectively (2.8–3.7× improvement). (B) F1 Event (IoU-F1@0.5 with class matching): DANCE reaches 39.8%, while all three baselines remain near zero (0.0%, 0.1%, 2.5%). Error bars denote SEM across 9 subjects. These results confirm that classification models, whether designed for single-window or per-timestep prediction, cannot be trivially repurposed for continuous event detection.

Figure R5 — No sign of overfitting for CBraMod model on BI2014b

Figure R5. F1 sample is stable during validation until the end of the training. It shows no sign of overfitting on the smallest dataset BI2014b. F1 sample at validation is displayed across epochs on 5 different folds representing all subjects in the dataset.

Figure R6 — DETR loss is stable across different weights combination

Figure R6. Performance of the DANCE model is stable across different weights combination. Scores are averaged across three datasets (BI2014b, BNCI2014, Broderick2019) using per-subject metrics aggregated over 5-fold cross-validation. Error bars denote standard error of the mean across datasets.

Figure R7 — DANCE outperforms the other methods on motor imagery datasets

Figure R7. DANCE shows precise performance on both localization and classification. Qualitative comparison of decoded event timelines on two motor imagery BCI datasets. Top: BNCI2014 (4-class: Tongue, Feet, Left Hand, Right Hand) showing a 70 s excerpt from a held-out test session. Bottom: BNCI2015 (2-class: Feet, Right Hand) showing a 300 s excerpt, illustrating performance at a longer time scale. Each row displays the ground-truth (GT) events and predictions from DANCE, CBraMod, and U-Net. Solid borders denote correct detections (prediction overlaps a same-class GT event by more than 50%), while dashed borders indicate false positives. Event-level F1 (F1e) and sample-level F1 (F1s) are reported on the right margin, computed over the full timeline.

Table R3 — DANCE vs. Foundation Models

BackboneBI2014bBNCI2014Broderick
CBraMod.098.057.119
CBraMod†.096.212.145
LaBraM.099.147.056
LaBraM†.122.220.043
REVE.116.214.041
REVE†.108.288.044
DANCE.392.398.446

† = pretrained weights. Bold = best, underline = second best per column.

Table R3. Dense prediction with foundation backbones lags far behind DANCE. Each backbone (CBraMod, LaBraM, REVE) is trained from scratch or initialised from public pretrained weights and topped with a per-timestep MLP head. We report F1event (class-aware, IoU ≥ 0.5) averaged over 5-fold cross-validation on three datasets. We use the public default configuration of the foundation models.

Table R4 — DANCE vs. Foundation models with Perceiver+Decoder

BackboneBI2014bBNCI2014Broderick
CBraMod.349.205.454
CBraMod†.391.303.447
LaBraM.331.203.414
LaBraM†.373.207.428
REVE.374.236.416
REVE†.366.294.442
DANCE.392.398.446

† = pretrained weights. Bold = best, underline = second best per column.

Table R4. Adding a set-prediction decoder closes the gap, but DANCE still leads on most benchmarks. Same backbones as Table R1, now equipped with a Perceiver cross-attention module followed by a DETR-style bipartite-matching decoder. We report F1event (class-aware, IoU ≥ 0.5) averaged over 5-fold cross-validation. We use the public default configuration of the foundation models.
